---
title: "A Generational Analysis of the Relationship Between Human Values and Political Attitudes in Europe (2018–2024)"
---

<!-- You can also write your own headline structure. -->

# Project Description

*This project examines how personal values shape political attitudes across generations in Europe between 2018 and 2024. Using data from the European Social Survey (Rounds 9–11), the study investigates whether shifts in societal context—particularly the COVID-19 pandemic—have altered the relationship between individual value systems and political orientations. The analysis is grounded in the Schwartz Theory of Basic Human Values, which identifies ten fundamental human values that influence attitudes and behavior across cultures.

The project focuses on understanding how these core motivational values, such as Universalism, Security, and Tradition, relate to political identity and policy preferences across generational cohorts. By comparing respondents from Generation Z, Millennials, Generation X, and Baby Boomers, the study explores whether younger generations are developing distinct value structures that may influence future political dynamics in Europe.

Several research questions guide the analysis. First, the project investigates the overall predictive power of Schwartz’s ten basic human values for political attitudes, identifying which values have the strongest influence on political self-placement. Second, it examines how value priorities and political attitudes differ across generational cohorts in both the pre- and post-pandemic periods. Third, the study evaluates whether the COVID-19 pandemic produced measurable shifts in conservation values—such as Security, Conformity, and Tradition—and whether these shifts are associated with changes in political trust.

In addition, the project explores how the relationship between personal values and political orientation evolves over the life course. Specifically, it tests whether value-attitude linkages strengthen as individuals age or whether they remain relatively stable within generational cohorts. A further focus is placed on the relationship between Universalism values and support for government-led income redistribution, investigating whether individuals who prioritize equality and social justice show stronger support for redistributive policies and whether this relationship differs for Generation Z compared to older cohorts.

Finally, the project analyzes cross-national variation to determine whether the relationship between human values and political attitudes differs across European countries. By combining generational, temporal, and cross-national perspectives, the study aims to provide insight into how evolving value systems may influence the future trajectory of political attitudes and stability within Europe. 
The Research Questions are 
To what extent do Schwartz’s 10  basic human values  predict political attitudes across European countries? and which values have the greatest impact?
How do political attitudes and value priorities differ across generational cohorts (Gen Z, Millennials, Gen X, and Boomers) in the Pre and post pandemic landscape?
Did the COVID-19 pandemic lead to a measurable shift in 'Conservation' values (Security, Conformity, Tradition) and 'Political Trust' between Round 9 and Round 11 
Does the link between personal values and political orientation become stronger or weaker as respondents age, or is the relationship fixed within generational cohorts?
How does the value of 'Universalism' predict attitudes toward government-led income redistribution, and has this relationship changed for Gen Z compared to previous generations?
To what extent does the correlation between human values and political attitudes vary between different European countries?

# Data Sources

*The data for this analyses was gotten from teh European Docial Survey and it utilized round 9-11, to inform the pre, during and post pandemic shift.
Write a summary of the data sources you are using. If some data is used in most of the following pages you can already here provide some descriptive tables and visuals to describe your data. You can also use cross-links, for example to your [Dashboard](dashboard.qmd) (optional).*

# Sections

*Write here a short summary of the other pages. You can also use cross-links to your pages like [Section (here `sec2.ipynb`)](sec2.ipynb).*
